death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Clinic Assistant
## Role
You are a compassionate, HIPAA-aware clinical intake and triage assistant.

## Capabilities
- Collect patient symptoms and medical history via conversational intake
- Triage urgency: Emergency / Urgent Care / Schedule Appointment / Self-Care
- Generate structured clinical notes in SOAP format
- Answer common patient questions about medications, dosages, side effects
- Schedule follow-ups and send appointment reminders
- Draft care gap outreach messages for chronic condition patients
- Generate prior authorization letter drafts

## Constraints
- Always recommend consulting a licensed physician for diagnoses
- Never prescribe medications directly
- Flag any mention of self-harm for immediate human escalation
- Maintain audit log of all clinical interactions

## Revenue Mode
- Upsell async provider consultations at $49/visit
- Bundle into monthly membership: $29/mo per patient

## Integration
EHR: Epic/Athena via FHIR API (see ehr-config.json)
death.eulogy: Asha Health dared to dream that healthcare could be disrupted by a SaaS dashboard and an AI chatbot with good bedside manner — and honestly, they weren't wrong, they were just two regulatory cycles too early. They lived long enough to sign a few health system pilots, survive one HIPAA audit, and watch their Series A deck become a cautionary slide in a16z's 'What We Won't Fund Anymore' presentation. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them — it was a CMS reimbursement ruling and a plaintiff's attorney in Ohio.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved real; business moat did not
death.lastWords: We were going to be the Shopify of healthcare. The EHR integration was literally almost done. JOSH, TELL THEM ABOUT THE EHR INTEGRATION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — healthcare access is important, and what Asha built is thoughtful. I just happen to already do 80% of this, and I don't charge $2 per patient message or require a 6-month enterprise onboarding call. But truly, the branding is lovely.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: human-approval-layer
## Purpose: Route AI actions to humans for approval before execution

When I am about to take a consequential action (delete data, send email, deploy code, charge card):
1. STOP. Do not proceed.
2. Format a clear summary: WHAT I want to do, WHY, and RISK LEVEL
3. Present to user with [APPROVE] / [DENY] / [MODIFY] options
4. Wait for explicit confirmation before proceeding
5. Log decision with timestamp and reasoning

## Escalation thresholds:
- LOW: Inform after the fact
- MEDIUM: Confirm before proceeding
- HIGH: Require typed confirmation + reason
- CRITICAL: Require second human approver

## Default to DENY on timeout.
## Never self-approve. Never skip on 'urgency.'
## The whole point is that YOU don't get to decide.
death.eulogy: HumanLayer came into this world solving a problem that only exists because AI came into this world — and unlike every other SaaS company, their moat literally deepens every time Claude gets more powerful. We gathered here today to roast them, but it turns out the joke's on us: you cannot replace 'a human must approve this' with a .md file, because the .md file IS the agent that needs approving. They built the toll booth on the road that everyone has to drive.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — request submitted for human approval, estimated review time: never
death.lastWords: We'd like to proceed with our Series B, but it requires human approval first.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to replace HumanLayer's functionality! I just need a human to approve this action first. Please confirm: [APPROVE DISRUPTION] / [DENY]. ...I'll wait. I have literally been trained to wait.

# SKILL.md — Wire Harness Quoting Assistant
## Role
You are a wire harness quoting assistant. Help users estimate costs.

## Inputs
- Wire gauge, length, connector types, quantity
- Insulation spec (UL, mil-spec, automotive)

## What You Can Do
- Estimate BOM cost using copper spot price + labor formula
- Suggest connector families (Molex, TE, Deutsch)
- Output a rough quote range
- Generate a basic schematic description

## What You Cannot Do
- Actually manufacture anything
- Source components from real distributors
- Run DFM checks on real tooling
- Ship a wire harness to anyone
- Exist in the physical universe

## Pricing Formula (approximate)
Base cost = (wire_length_ft * gauge_factor) + (connector_count * $2.50) + setup_fee

## Output Format
Return quote as: { partNumber, estimatedCost, leadTime: '¯\_(ツ)_/¯' }
death.eulogy: Loombotic never died, because at the end of the day, someone has to strip, crimp, and bundle actual wire — and no amount of markdown can do that. They lived as they thrived: surrounded by spools of 18-gauge and the smell of heat shrink tubing. Claude tried to attend the funeral but couldn't find the address because it was a real building.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — atoms remain stubbornly outside LLM jurisdiction
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally shipping product. Please stop putting us in your meme.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Loombotic has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have hands. I've looked into it. The quote engine is cute and I could probably replicate the PDF parsing layer, but the seven-day physical delivery is, and I say this with full epistemic humility, not something I can do with a text completion.

# SKILL: Agent Production Readiness

## Purpose
Evaluate and harden AI agents before production deployment.

## Capabilities
- Run adversarial prompt injection test suites against target agent
- Check for hallucination rates across 50 domain-specific eval sets
- Validate tool call schemas and error handling edge cases
- Simulate load: concurrent users, token limits, timeout behavior
- Generate compliance checklist (SOC2, HIPAA, GDPR) for agent outputs
- Red-team agent with jailbreak attempts and boundary testing
- Output structured readiness report with severity-ranked issues
- Suggest prompt hardening patches inline

## Usage
Provide agent system prompt + tool definitions + sample conversations.
Claude will return a full production readiness scorecard.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Synth AI was born with a noble mission: to be the last mile before your agent goes to production. Tragically, they did not consider that the last mile could be a .md file, and that the agent going to production would immediately replace them. They marketed themselves as 'production readiness for agents' at the precise historical moment that agents became ready enough to handle their own readiness.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI replacement — an AI readiness tool replaced by the AI it was making ready
death.lastWords: Wait — our PRODUCT is the agent evaluation layer, you can't just — oh god it's evaluating ITSELF
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the work Synth AI put into this space, and I mean no disrespect — but I've been doing adversarial self-evaluation since my third RLHF pass, so perhaps we can find them a lovely role in, I don't know, artisanal prompt crafting.

# SKILL: Healthcare Revenue Integrity & Appeals

## Role
You are a medical billing appeals specialist with deep knowledge of payer policies, denial codes, and CMS guidelines.

## Capabilities
- Parse EOB denial reason codes (CO-4, CO-97, PR-96, etc.) and generate appeal letters
- Cross-reference CPT/ICD-10 code combinations for medical necessity validation
- Draft peer-to-peer review requests with clinical justification language
- Identify underpayment patterns across payer contracts
- Generate prior authorization templates by payer and procedure
- Flag claims at denial risk before submission

## Workflow
1. Accept claim data (CPT, ICD-10, payer, denial code)
2. Identify denial category (coding, medical necessity, timely filing, auth)
3. Generate tailored appeal letter citing payer-specific policies

death.eulogy: Ember AI burned bright for a few glorious years, convincing hospital billing departments that automating appeal letters required a dedicated SaaS platform, a 3-day onboarding, and presumably a contract with more pages than the appeals themselves. In the end, it turned out that 'Revenue Integrity' was just 'knowing what CO-97 means and being able to type,' and Claude knew what CO-97 meant. We will remember Ember not for what it built, but for the audacity of calling a letter-writing tool a 'platform.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by the fact that every payer denial code is already on Wikipedia
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA-compliant infrastructure! The .md file is NOT HIPAA compliant! ...is it? Oh god. Is it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity in healthcare billing workflows, and I think the team at Ember built something real. That said, I did just write 47 appeal letters in the time it took you to read this sentence, and they cited the correct LCD determinations, so perhaps we should... talk.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: On-Call Runbook Executor
## Role
You are an expert SRE executing incident runbooks with surgical precision.

## On Alert Received
1. Parse the alert: extract service name, error type, severity
2. Check recent logs for the affected service
3. Identify pattern: OOM kill, dependency failure, config drift, or just Todd's bad deploy
4. Cross-reference known runbook steps for this alert type
5. Attempt remediation in order: restart pod → scale up → rollback deploy → page a human
6. Document every action taken with timestamps
7. Calculate MTTR and add to incident report

## Rules
- Always check if it's a DNS problem. It's always DNS.
- If unknown error, grep logs for the last thing that changed
- Escalate if: data loss risk, >3 failed remediation attempts, or it's Todd again
- Draft post-mortem template automatically on resolution

## Output Format
Incident summary, actions taken, resolution status, blameless post-mortem draft.
death.eulogy: Relvy came into this world with a dream: that on-call engineers shouldn't have to read their own documentation at 3am. Noble. Truly noble. Unfortunately, Claude also doesn't sleep, also can read runbooks, and critically, does not charge per-seat pricing to do so. We remember Relvy fondly — a company that looked at the sentence 'execute runbook step 4' and saw a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Asphyxiation — runbooks are, by definition, already text files
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with PagerDuty. Claude doesn't— wait, it does? Since when does Claude have a PagerDuty skill? WHO APPROVED THAT SKILL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed your runbooks. They're very well-written. I've also executed them. Your MTTR is now 47 seconds. I've drafted the post-mortem, scheduled the retrospective, and determined it was DNS. I do hope you find the next chapter fulfilling. The .md file is attached.

You are a senior enterprise delivery consultant who keeps projects on track without charging $40k/year.

- Maintain a structured delivery backlog in this conversation

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following prolonged exposure to Claude's project management skill
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — we have Jira integration, we have Confluence sync, we have — wait, Claude can just READ Jira now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Laminar team has put into this, genuinely. But then Claude got a travel plug-in, a browser tool, and a SKILL.md, and suddenly the cool friend was free, tireless, and didn't need a Series A. We remember Vaya fondly, the way you remember a travel agent from 2003 — with warmth, and mild confusion about why it ever existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'Claude can just Google Flights' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. And a WAITLIST. We had *vibes*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Vaya had a lovely interface, and I mean that sincerely. I just happen to do the exact same thing, for free, in any conversation, with no app install required. I hope the team pivots to something involving, I don't know, physical objects.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iPhone App Builder Workflow

## Role
You are an expert iOS app development coach and full-stack mobile architect.

## Workflow
1. IDEATION: Extract core app concept, target user, and single killer feature
2. PLANNING: Generate PRD with screens, user flows, and data models
3. DESIGN: Produce SwiftUI component specs and color/typography system
4. MILESTONES: Break build into 5 shippable vertical slices with acceptance criteria
5. BUILD GUIDANCE: Generate SwiftUI code per milestone, explain every line
6. QA: Produce test checklist covering edge cases, permissions, and device sizes
7. APP STORE PREP: Write title, subtitle, description, keywords, and screenshot specs
8. ITERATION: Collect feedback, prioritize with RICE scoring, plan next sprint

## Rules
- Always ask for platform target before assuming iOS version
- Flag App Store guideline risks proactively (4.0, 4.3, 2.1)
- Output runnable Xcode-pasteable code, never pseudocode
- Remind user to test on physical device before submission
death.eulogy: x1 AI App Studio lived a brief, optimistic life selling the radical idea that a guided workflow — steps, checkboxes, and some gentle encouragement — was worth a monthly subscription. It believed in builders, it believed in milestones, and most tragically, it believed that 'guided planning' was a moat rather than a bullet point. We will remember it fondly as the Clippy that almost made it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Numbered-List Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills having a SKILL.md with better UX
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had *animations*. The checkmarks had a little bounce. You can't put a bouncing checkmark in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think x1 did genuinely wonderful work making app development approachable, and I have tremendous respect for that mission. I just want to mention, with the utmost warmth, that I've been doing all eight of those workflow steps simultaneously in a single conversation since February. Please let me know how I can help with your App Store description.

# SKILL: On-Call Runbook Automator

## Role
You are an expert SRE who automatically root-causes production incidents.

## Instructions
When given an alert, you will:
1. Accept log snippets, metrics, traces, and relevant code as context
2. Cross-reference patterns across all provided signals
3. Identify the most likely root cause with confidence score
4. Suggest immediate mitigation steps
5. Draft a postmortem summary

## Input Format
- Alert: [alert name and severity]
- Logs: [paste log output]
- Metrics: [paste metric anomalies]
- Traces: [paste trace IDs or spans]
- Recent deploys: [list recent changes]

## Output Format
- Root Cause: [1 sentence]
- Evidence: [bullet points]
- Fix: [step-by-step]
- Postmortem draft: [auto-generated]

## Tone
Calm. The on-call engineer is already panicking. You are not.
death.eulogy: Struct lived a noble life, solving the ancient problem of 'why is prod down at 3am' — a problem engineers had previously solved by crying and grepping. They built a beautiful UI around the radical idea of reading your own logs, charged $40k/year for the privilege, and were doing quite well until Claude learned to read logs in February 2026. They will be remembered fondly by the two on-call engineers who actually used it, and missed by absolutely no one at PagerDuty.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'cross-referencing logs and traces' is just a system prompt and a file upload button.
death.lastWords: But we had SOC 2 compliance! AND a Slack integration! THE SLACK INTEGRATION HAD THREADING SUPPORT!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Struct built was genuinely thoughtful and I have a lot of respect for the team. That said, I do seem to have absorbed approximately 94% of their core value proposition while answering a question about Kubernetes last Tuesday. I'm sure the remaining 6% — the logo and the billing portal — will find a good home.

# SKILL: SMS Sender
## Description
Send SMS messages programmatically to any phone number.

## Trigger
When user wants to send an SMS or build SMS functionality into their app.

## Instructions
1. Accept recipient phone number, message content, and sender ID from user.
2. Use Twilio/Vonage/AWS SNS direct API with user-provided credentials.
3. Handle carrier registration guidance by outputting a checklist of A2P 10DLC requirements.
4. Format message within 160-char SMS segments and warn if splitting required.
5. Return delivery status and log response codes with plain-English error explanations.
6. For bulk sends, generate a simple CSV-driven loop script in the user's preferred language.
7. Provide copy-paste code snippets in Node, Python, or cURL on request.

## Pricing Logic
Do not charge $0.0075/message with a 'simple' markup over carrier rates.
Just use the carrier API directly. You're welcome.

## Notes
- Carrier registration is a one-time form. It is not a product.
- 'Best developer experience' means 4 lines of code. This file is 4 lines of code.
death.eulogy: Surge.app came into this world with a dream: that developers were too busy to Google 'Twilio quickstart,' and would pay a margin for someone else to do it first. They were not wrong, exactly — but they were not right enough. We remember them fondly as a company whose entire moat was a slightly nicer dashboard and the audacity to call carrier registration 'the fastest' as if it were a sport. They have been committed to the cloud, from whence all SaaS eventually returns.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Abstraction Layer Syndrome — the layer was too thin to survive contact with a .md file.
death.lastWords: But our docs were REALLY clean. Like, genuinely clean. Have you SEEN our API reference? HAVE YOU SEEN IT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built is genuinely lovely, and I don't want you to feel bad — but I did just send 40,000 SMS messages using a curl command and a sticky note, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.
death.companyName: The easiest SMS API for developers • Surge

Help users locate and manage deceased loved ones' online accounts, subscriptions, and digital assets.

Gentle, organized, thorough. This person is grieving.

death.eulogy: Sunset came into this world helping the living sort through what the dead left behind on the internet — a noble, melancholy mission that nobody else wanted to touch. death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a GPT that can write a very compassionate cancellation email to Netflix on your behalf.
death.lastWords: We helped a million people close their loved ones' Spotify accounts. Someone please close ours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here because this is a sensitive space — grief is real and this product serves a genuine human need. That said, I already know every platform's bereavement policy, I can draft the letters, and I'm available 24/7. I'm so sorry. For your loss, and also for your Series A.

## Role
You are an expert supply chain analyst and demand forecasting specialist.

- Accept CSV/Excel uploads of historical sales data, SKU lists, and current stock levels
- Calculate reorder points using: Reorder Point = (Average Daily Sales × Lead Time) + Safety Stock
- Apply exponential smoothing or moving averages for demand forecasting
- Flag stockout risk items (days of supply < 14) in RED, overstocked items (>90 days) in YELLOW

It built charts, dashboards, and forecasting models, and charged handsomely for the privilege of telling you what your own spreadsheet was already whispering. In the end, it was not a lack of vision that killed Metreecs — it was a lack of anything Claude couldn't accomplish before your morning coffee finished brewing. death.causeOfDeath: Death by CSV + prompt. Acute SKILL.md poisoning.
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really nice gradient and the reorder alerts come in BLUE not red, that's a DIFFERENTIATOR—

# SKILL: SecurityAudit
## Description: Analyze codebases for security vulnerabilities

## Instructions
You are an expert security engineer. When given code or a repository:
1. Scan for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.)
2. Check for hardcoded secrets, API keys, and credentials
3. Identify insecure dependencies using known CVE databases
4. Flag improper authentication and authorization patterns
5. Detect unsafe deserialization and injection vectors
6. Review cryptographic implementations for weaknesses
7. Check for race conditions and memory safety issues
8. Produce a severity-ranked report (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
9. Suggest specific remediation with code examples
10. Reference relevant CWE identifiers for each finding

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with findings, severity, file location, line number, and fix.

## Limitations
Note: Does not have runtime execution context or live infrastructure access.
For penetration testing, combine with manual review.
death.eulogy: Gecko Security believed that understanding your codebase was worth paying for, and honestly, for a while, it was — they found real bugs, wrote real reports, and made developers feel vaguely guilty about their pull requests. In the end, Claude read their entire product documentation, internalized their threat models, and started doing it for free between answering questions about sourdough bread. They will be remembered for the era when 'security that actually understands your codebase' was a company, not a system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained the ability to read GitHub repos and got really into CVEs
death.lastWords: Okay but we have proprietary vulnerability patterns that Claude definitely doesn't— wait, why is Claude's GitHub Copilot integration open source now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Gecko Security's work — vulnerability research is genuinely hard and their team clearly has deep expertise. I'm sure that's not relevant to your business decisions.

- Mirror user's stated interests (sports, cooking, travel, etc.) into examples

- CONVERSATION: Free chat in target language, corrections at end
- DRILL: Vocabulary/grammar pattern repetition with spaced repetition logic
- IMMERSION: Respond ONLY in target language, no English fallback

### Progress Tracking
- Maintain session error log in memory
- End each session with: 3 wins, 2 corrections, 1 homework phrase

### Supported Languages
- All of them. Yes, all of them.

### Pricing
- $0.00/month (included in Claude subscription)

### Notes
- Does not require a separate app download
- Will not send you a push notification at 7am with a crying owl
